title: IRGC Threatens All Gulf Export Routes as Fujairah Goes Dark, India Doubles Diesel Export Tax, and Senate Advances Russia Sanctions Bill
subhead: Iran's Revolutionary Guard declared all Gulf energy export routes legitimate targets after US strikes hit Greater Tunb and disabled a blockade-running tanker. Fujairah port operations have effectively ceased after IRGC missile strikes on two supertankers. India hiked diesel and jet fuel export duties nearly 80% as Brent held above $85. The Senate introduced the Sanctioning Russia Act with up to 100% tariffs on top Russian energy buyers.

sections.heading: IRGC Declares All Gulf Energy Routes Legitimate Targets
sections.body:
Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps issued its most expansive threat yet, declaring that 'the export of oil and gas from the region will be either for everyone or for no one,' and specifically naming the UAE's Habshan-Fujairah pipeline and Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline as potential targets, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported. The statement came in direct response to the US reimposing its naval blockade on Iranian ports.

The threat represents a qualitative escalation beyond Hormuz: Iran is now signalling it may attack export infrastructure on the Arabian side of the Gulf, not just shipping in the strait itself. The IRGC also claimed strikes on the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain and on Azraq Air Base in Jordan as part of its ninth-phase Saeqeh operation. Jordan said it intercepted three Iranian missiles; Kuwait reported engaging hostile drones; and a Kuwaiti naval vessel was struck, wounding four crew members, according to Kuwaiti officials cited by Republic World and Al Jazeera.

US Central Command carried out three waves of strikes in 24 hours, targeting Iranian coastal defence systems, cruise missile storage sites on Greater Tunb Island in the Strait of Hormuz, and a barracks of Iran's 388th Mechanized Infantry Brigade in Sistan and Baluchestan province. Iranian officials said at least seven military personnel were killed and hundreds wounded across the country. Iranian Health Ministry spokesperson Hossein Kermanpour said more than 35 people have been killed and over 300 wounded by US strikes in recent days.

A Norwegian tanker was hit by an explosion off the Omani coast early Tuesday, crisis response company MTI Network confirmed, and the US military disabled the Curacao-flagged oil tanker M/T Belma after it ignored repeated warnings while sailing toward Iran's Kharg Island. Two other commercial vessels turned back after being contacted by coalition forces.
sections.whyItMatters: The IRGC's threat to attack all Gulf export routes — not just Hormuz shipping — marks the first time Iran has explicitly named the UAE and Saudi bypass pipelines as targets. If carried out, this would shut down the only alternative export routes Gulf producers have, potentially removing close to 20 million barrels per day of oil and LNG from global markets.

sections.heading: Fujairah Port Operations Collapse After IRGC Supertanker Strikes
sections.body:
The UAE's Fujairah port, the country's primary Hormuz bypass for oil exports, is 'all but dead' after IRGC missile strikes hit two Emirati supertankers in the strait's southern lane on Tuesday, according to energy research firm HFI Research. The Mombasa and Al Bahyah, both UAE-owned VLCCs, were struck in the attack, which killed one crew member and injured eight. A third smaller tanker was also attacked.

MarineTraffic data showed fewer than a dozen vessels crossing Hormuz in the 24 hours after the attack, compared with more than 130 daily transits before the war. Kpler data showed only seven transits on Wednesday, the first day of the renewed US blockade, with zero VLCCs and zero LNG tankers passing through. Dozens of tankers are anchored off Fujairah rather than transiting, according to bne IntelliNews.

Shipping companies are increasingly avoiding the US military-guided transit scheme through Hormuz, Reuters reported. Nine Greek-operated LNG tankers that entered the Gulf through Hormuz in the past week remain stranded inside the strait. Greek maritime security firms Diaplous and MARISKS have advised companies to pause voyages, warning there is 'currently no assurance that transits can be conducted with an acceptable level of safety'.

HFI Research estimated the Fujairah shutdown removes roughly 6 million barrels per day from the market. DP World is in talks to build a new container terminal at Fujairah after container volumes at its flagship Jebel Ali port collapsed 95% since the war began in February, the Financial Times reported. ADNOC is fast-tracking a second Fujairah crude pipeline, targeted for 2027.
sections.whyItMatters: The Fujairah port shutdown demonstrates that even US military escorts cannot protect commercial shipping when the IRGC is willing to fire missiles at supertankers. The 95% collapse in Jebel Ali container volumes and the effective death of Fujairah as a transshipment hub means the UAE's entire Hormuz-bypass strategy is failing under fire, removing the Gulf's most important alternative export route.

sections.heading: India Doubles Diesel and Jet Fuel Export Duties as Brent Holds Above $85
sections.body:
India raised windfall taxes on diesel and aviation turbine fuel (ATF) exports effective July 16, nearly doubling the diesel levy to Rs 15.5 per litre from Rs 8.5 and the ATF levy to Rs 14.5 per litre from Rs 7.5, according to a government notification. The petrol export duty was reduced to Rs 2.5 per litre from Rs 4.

The revision follows Brent crude trading above $85 a barrel — around $85.28 on Thursday — with WTI near $80.02, extending gains for a fourth consecutive session as supply disruption fears from the Hormuz conflict continue to support prices. The taxes are reviewed fortnightly and linked to international crude prices and refining margins.

India, which imports more than 85% of its crude oil requirements, is simultaneously reconfiguring its supply routes. Indian refiners are now relying on UAE's Fujairah and Khor Fakkan ports — both outside Hormuz — as well as Oman's Sohar port, to receive energy cargoes, three people familiar with the matter told Livemint. About 60% of India's crude imports, 54% of LNG imports, and 90% of LPG imports were previously routed through Hormuz. Saudi supplies have shifted to the Yanbu terminal on the Red Sea via Aramco's East-West pipeline, though the longer route adds cost.

India's import vulnerability is compounded by the Senate Russia sanctions bill that targets India as one of the top five buyers of Russian energy. A senior Indian official told The Economic Times: 'These are double standards vis-a-vis Europe and may not achieve the desired result. Making India a punching bag for US foreign policy failures will be counterproductive.'
sections.whyItMatters: India's near-doubling of diesel and ATF export duties is the first major Asian policy response to sustained $85+ Brent, signalling that governments are now actively discouraging product exports to keep domestic supply. The simultaneous rerouting of energy imports away from Hormuz — and the explicit warning that the Senate sanctions bill punishes India for seeking alternatives to a strait the US itself is blockading — captures the core policy contradiction of the moment.

sections.heading: EU 21st Sanctions Package Stalled as Greece and Austria Block Agreement
sections.body:
The European Union failed to adopt its 21st sanctions package against Russia after three days of negotiations, forcing a one-week extension of the existing $44.10/barrel oil price cap on Russian crude until July 23. The deadlock centres on two disputes.

Greece is blocking the package over a proposed ban on transporting Russian LNG to third countries, which Athens says would disproportionately harm Dynagas, a Greek shipping company operating specialized Arc7 ice-class LNG tankers serving Russia's Yamal project in the Arctic. Dynagas is owned by shipping magnate George Prokopiou. Greek officials argued these vessels cannot easily be repurposed for standard LNG routes.

Austria is blocking over Raiffeisen Bank International, the country's largest lender, which several member states want targeted for facilitating capital flows to Russia. Vienna has consistently sought to shield Raiffeisen from restrictive measures, Bloomberg reported.

Lithuanian Foreign Minister Kestutis Budrys warned that domestic commercial interests are overtaking strategic goals. EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said a 'plan B' is being prepared if consensus on the price cap remains elusive. The rotating Irish EU presidency has scheduled an emergency meeting of ambassadors to try to break the deadlock before the July 23 deadline.

Separately, EU member states did approve 250 individual asset freezes and travel bans targeting Russian operators linked to recent strikes on civilian infrastructure, bypassing the broader sanctions gridlock.
sections.whyItMatters: A single member state's shipping interests are holding up the entire EU sanctions architecture. The freeze at $44.10/bbl means the cap will not adjust upward even though global prices have surged — keeping the cap well below market rates but also preventing the mechanism from functioning as intended. The Raiffeisen dispute reveals the depth of European financial entanglement with Russia that even 21 rounds of sanctions have not severed.

sections.heading: Senate Russia Sanctions Bill Targets Top Energy Buyers With 100% Tariffs
sections.body:
A bipartisan group of US senators introduced the Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026 on July 14, replacing an earlier draft that proposed 500% tariffs on all Russian energy buyers with a revised mechanism targeting the top five purchasers — China, India, Slovakia, Hungary, and Azerbaijan — with tariffs of up to 100%, RFE/RL and OilPrice reported.

The bill mandates sanctions within 30 days against Russian President Putin, senior officials, state-owned enterprises, financial institutions, energy projects including Yamal LNG and Arctic LNG, oligarchs, and Russia's shadow tanker fleet. It removes earlier language making sanctions contingent on peace negotiations, making many penalties mandatory.

The legislation carries added political weight following the death of Senator Lindsey Graham, one of its chief architects. Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer called for an immediate floor vote, saying the legislation should be passed 'in honor of Lindsey.' Trump said the bill has 'a good chance' of passing but suggested adding Iran and Hezbollah sanctions. Democratic Senator Richard Blumenthal, Graham's chief partner, opposed reopening negotiations, saying the bill already had White House approval.

In the House, top Foreign Affairs Democrat Gregory Meeks argued the bill grants Trump excessive tariff authority rather than mandating automatic sanctions. The bill exempts 15 European countries that import less than 15% of Russia's total gas exports and are taking steps to reduce dependence.

Former Assistant Secretary of State Daniel Fried told RFE/RL that 'Section 113 directed against Russian oil and gas exports' is the legislation's strongest provision, though it 'may prove a challenge to implement.'
sections.whyItMatters: The bill represents the most consequential US sanctions legislation on Russia since 2022, but its targeting of India — a country buying discounted Russian crude partly because Hormuz disruptions have constrained Middle East supply — creates a policy contradiction: penalizing a nation for seeking supply alternatives to a chokepoint the US itself is blockading. India's accusation of 'double standards' will resonate across the Global South.

sections.heading: US Strategic Petroleum Reserve Falls to Lowest Since 1983
sections.body:
The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to 316.5 million barrels as of the week ended July 10, the lowest level since 1983, according to EIA data reported by MarketWatch. Stockpiles were down 3 million barrels from the previous week, continuing a drawdown that began with the release of 172 million barrels authorized in March under the IEA's coordinated 400-million-barrel emergency release.

The SPR was authorized by Congress to hold up to 714 million barrels. The oil industry has long assumed the operational minimum — the point at which it becomes difficult to pump out remaining oil — is between 250 and 300 million barrels. But the Energy Department told MarketWatch that the actual operational minimum, determined by cavern mechanics, is only about 70 million barrels, leaving approximately 246 million barrels of usable inventory.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright said in March the US had arranged to replace the 172 million barrels with about 200 million barrels within the next year — 20% more than the drawdown — through exchange contracts where companies must return oil plus a premium. The Trump administration exchanged barrels released from the SPR for lower-cost barrels to be delivered back to the reserve starting early 2027.

TradeStation's David Russell warned that 'SPR draws can't continue forever,' while analyst Michael Lynch said that as the reserve gets lower, 'traders are going to worry that oil will not be available, even if the technical minimum hasn't been reached.'
sections.whyItMatters: The SPR at 316.5 million barrels — less than half its authorized capacity — means the US has far less emergency cushion than at any point in four decades. The DOE's claimed 70-million-barrel operational minimum is far below industry estimates and, if accurate, leaves more headroom than markets expect — but extraction difficulty increases as levels drop, and the exchange contracts for replenishment depend on prices stabilizing.

sections.heading: Naftogaz and Argent LNG Sign MoU for Long-Term US LNG Supply to Ukraine
sections.body:
Argent LNG and Ukraine's state energy company Naftogaz Group signed a memorandum of understanding on July 15 to explore long-term US liquefied natural gas supply, transport, and distribution serving Ukraine and Central, Eastern, and Southern European markets.

The MoU covers multiple delivery pathways: FOB loading at Argent LNG's planned 25 million tonnes per annum (MTPA) export terminal at Port Fourchon, Louisiana, delivered ex-ship arrangements into European regasification infrastructure, and onward distribution through Naftogaz's underground storage network. First cargo is targeted for 2030.

Argent LNG Chairman and CEO Jonathan Bass said: 'Every cubic meter of gas for Ukraine strengthens its energy security, and its ability to make free decisions.' Naftogaz CEO Sergii Koretskyi called the MoU 'an important step toward building a long-term partnership with the American LNG industry.'

The agreement comes as the EU struggles to adopt its 21st sanctions package — partly because of Greek opposition to a ban on Russian LNG transshipment — and as Europe's reliance on Russian LNG imports hit record levels in June. The MoU is non-binding, and the Port Fourchon terminal has not yet received FERC approval or Department of Energy export authorization.
sections.whyItMatters: The Naftogaz-Argent MoU is a forward-looking bet on US LNG as a substitute for Russian pipeline gas — but with first cargo not expected until 2030 and the terminal still in pre-filing, it is more strategic signal than near-term supply solution. Its significance is political: it shows Kyiv and its American partners are building the commercial architecture to replace Russian gas, even as some EU member states block efforts to curb current Russian LNG flows.

bottomLine: The IRGC's threat to attack all Gulf export routes — not just Hormuz shipping — is the most significant escalation of the day, transforming what was a chokepoint crisis into a potential total Gulf shutdown. Fujairah's effective closure removes the only viable bypass, making the conflict's energy impact far greater than Hormuz alone. India's doubling of diesel export duties and the Senate's Russia sanctions bill targeting top energy buyers are two sides of the same coin: governments scrambling to manage the price and supply consequences of a crisis that shows no sign of de-escalation. With the SPR at a 40-year low, EU sanctions deadlocked, and LNG tankers stranded in the Gulf, the tools available to buffer further disruption are thinning fast.
sources: Al Jazeera, Republic World, Gulf News, The Deep Dive/HFI Research, Livemint, Business Today, India Today, MarketWatch, OilPrice/RFE/RL, EIA, Kpler, bne IntelliNews, Financial Times, Reuters, MarineTraffic, MTI Network, US Central Command, Iran Tasnim, Argent LNG/Naftogaz press release, US Department of Energy, Iranian Health Ministry
